cross_module_architecture.strengths: Clean Blueprint decomposition with one Blueprint per domain (files, docker, scan, dump, memprocfs, auth). MCP and CLI layers are structurally separated. No circular imports detected.
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Two defects: database.py is a raw-connection hub imported by 6+ consumers; and the DooD path translation concern is implemented twice in separate modules with diverging logic.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: Blueprint structure is sound but the database hub and dual path-resolution implementations are repeated architectural gaps. Score reflects mostly clean boundaries with two concrete violations.

high_level_elegance.strengths: The project decomposes cleanly into CLI, API server (with blueprints), MCP, and Dockerfiles layers. Each blueprint has a clear domain (files, docker, scan, dump, memprocfs).
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Two ownership-clarity defects: api_server/auth.py and routes/auth.py have identical conceptual names with different roles; run_analysis() serves both CLI and API without differentiation.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: Solid structure for a tool of this complexity. Naming collision between middleware and blueprint creates navigability confusion. Score reflects generally predictable structure minus these two issues.

mid_level_elegance.strengths: Blueprints are registered cleanly in app.py. Background tasks correctly use threading.Thread. DB operations use a consistent get_db_connection() pattern.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Global runner_func injection is the primary seam defect — it creates an implicit boot-order contract. argparse.Namespace as cross-boundary data carrier is untyped and field-unsafe.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Seam design is functional but brittle. The runner_func global and Namespace carrier are both easy-to-hit problems that affect every scan path.

low_level_elegance.strengths: Short helper functions like calculate_sha256, cleanup_timeouts, get_file_hash, cleanup_container are well-scoped. memprocfs_server.py has clean separation of list/read/health endpoints.
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Several large functions mix multiple responsibilities: list_evidences (130+ lines, two DB connections, two filesystem passes), background_dump_task (110+ lines), run_analysis (200+ lines).
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The small utility functions are clean. The large orchestration functions accumulate too many responsibilities, making them hard to test and modify safely.

type_safety.strengths: utils.py uses Union[list, dict] and Optional annotations. Vol3RunConfig and Vol2RunConfig are dataclasses with typed fields. multi_volatility_base.py uses Optional and dataclass annotations.
type_safety.issue_character: Tests assert return types (None) that don't match annotations (str, dict). Route handlers and run_analysis lack annotations. Module-level untyped argparse passing undermines the typed dataclasses.
type_safety.score_rationale: Annotation coverage is partial — the dataclass layer is well-typed but the route/orchestration layer has almost no annotations. Three test contract mismatches indicate annotation drift.

error_consistency.strengths: Routes consistently return JSON error dicts with HTTP status codes. background tasks catch Exception and log errors before updating DB status.
error_consistency.issue_character: Two different error strategies within the same module (utils.py): get_file_hash raises, clean_and_parse_json returns error dict. memprocfs_server silently swallows VFS errors.
error_consistency.score_rationale: Route-level error handling is consistent. The intra-module inconsistency in utils.py and the broad exception swallowing in memprocfs_server are the main defects.

contract_coherence.strengths: get_file_hash has a docstring that accurately describes its raise behavior. Vol3RunConfig/Vol2RunConfig dataclasses have clear field contracts.
contract_coherence.issue_character: Three test/implementation contract mismatches; /health auth bypass not implemented despite test asserting it. check_authorization returns multiple incompatible types without annotation.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The contracts visible in annotations are mostly honored. However, the test-production divergence on three contracts and the /health bug are concrete proof of contract drift.

api_surface_coherence.strengths: Most route patterns follow /scans/<uuid>/sub-resource. Blueprint registration in app.py is clean. MCP tools have clear docstrings with usage instructions.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: URL inconsistency between /scan (POST, singular, no ID) and /status/<scan_id> vs /scans/<uuid> breaks the resource-oriented convention across the same blueprint.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: Mostly consistent API surface but the /status/<scan_id> outlier alongside /scans/<uuid> patterns creates an inconsistent client experience.

design_coherence.strengths: memprocfs_server.py is well-structured with clear route, VMM state, and background thread concerns separated. config.py is minimal and focused.
design_coherence.issue_character: run_analysis() and background_dump_task() are two large functions with multiple distinct responsibilities. A self-narrating TODO comment in execute_plugin confirms acknowledged design debt.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Individual small files are coherent. The large orchestration functions in multivol.py and dump.py are the primary design coherence failures.

naming_quality.strengths: Domain-specific naming throughout: ingest_results_to_db, cleanup_timeouts, process_recover_fs, resolve_host_path are all intention-revealing. Module names match their domains.
naming_quality.issue_character: check_authorization named as a predicate but functions as a guard returning response tuples. resolve_host_path and resolve_path serve the same purpose with different names.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming is generally good across the codebase. The two issues found are real but localized. Score reflects strong naming with two specific callouts.

convention_outlier.strengths: All route files use Blueprint pattern consistently. snake_case is used uniformly. Logging follows the same pattern (logging.info/warning/error) throughout.
convention_outlier.issue_character: request.json vs request.get_json() inconsistency across sibling route handlers. Two auth files with identical conceptual names but different roles.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Generally consistent conventions. The request.json/get_json() inconsistency affects all route handlers and has behavioral consequences. Naming collision is a navigation hazard.

abstraction_fitness.strengths: Vol3RunConfig/Vol2RunConfig dataclasses are appropriate abstractions for runner configuration. Blueprint separation is earned and well-bounded.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: strings.py instantiates a stateless class just to use two methods. Two parallel path resolution implementations indicate a missing shared abstraction.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Core abstractions are appropriate. The module-level singleton and dual path resolution are unnecessary complexity that point to a missing utility consolidation.

initialization_coupling.strengths: config.py is explicit about directory structure. ensure_dirs() is called once at startup. The DB is initialized in a controlled try/except block.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: Env vars captured at import time in config.py and MCP module. Module-level httpx client created with import-time token. runner_func global requires order-dependent init.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Import-time env var capture is the systemic issue — it affects testability and runtime flexibility. The httpx client issue is concrete (silent 'Bearer None' on missing token).

dependency_health.strengths: No duplicate libraries detected for the same purpose. pyyaml and requests are correctly listed.
dependency_health.issue_character: requirements.txt is critically incomplete: flask, flask-cors, docker, waitress, rich, httpx, fastmcp are all imported at runtime but not declared. setup.py references requirements.txt, making pip install produce an unusable package.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This is a severe infrastructure gap — installing the package via pip will fail at runtime because 7+ dependencies are missing from requirements.txt.

ai_generated_debt.strengths: Comments generally add context (explaining DooD path translation logic, Docker volume semantics, CORS configuration). No entry/exit logging on every function. No defensive null-checks on typed values.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: One clear case: the self-narrating development comment in execute_plugin. A few overly verbose inline comments that narrate obvious code.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase does not show heavy AI-generated debt patterns. The one development comment that should have been a TODO is the main finding.

package_organization.strengths: CLI/multivol/api_server/routes/ clearly groups HTTP endpoints. MCP/ is separate from CLI/. Dockerfiles/ separation is appropriate.
package_organization.issue_character: CLI/build/lib/ contains old production code that should not be in VCS. This is the primary organization defect.
package_organization.score_rationale: The live source structure is navigable and well-organized. The build directory artifact is the main issue — it creates a duplicate package tree that confuses search and navigation tools.

incomplete_migration.strengths: The current Blueprint architecture is a clean, complete replacement for the old monolithic api.py. No deprecated symbols are called by active code.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The old api.py monolith (1838 lines) remains in build/lib/. Three tests were written for an older API contract and now fail. execute_plugin has an acknowledged incomplete refactor.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Migration is functionally complete but artifacts remain. Failing tests and the acknowledged TODO in production code indicate the cleanup phase was not finished.

logic_clarity.strengths: Most route handlers have clear linear control flow. early returns on validation failures are used consistently. Background task logic in scan.py is straightforward.
logic_clarity.issue_character: The commands unbound variable risk in run_analysis() is a latent NameError on unexpected mode values. The /health auth bypass omission is a logic gap relative to stated intent (test documents the intent).
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Logic is generally clear. The commands unbound variable is a real latent bug. Most other control flow issues (like the /health auth) are documented separately as contract/auth issues.

authorization_consistency.strengths: All main API routes are protected via a single before_request hook. The login endpoint exemption is explicit.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: /health not exempted (monitoring cannot probe without credentials). memprocfs sidecar has no auth at all. Weak default credentials that ship with the tool.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The main API has consistent auth. The /health omission, unauthenticated sidecar, and weak defaults are three distinct authorization gaps that together represent a significant security surface.

test_strategy.strengths: conftest.py provides a clean, reusable Flask test client fixture. test_auth.py covers the auth middleware at a functional level. The test structure is extensible.
test_strategy.issue_character: Core scan execution, Docker management, and all route handlers (except auth) have zero coverage. Three tests actively fail against the current implementation.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Two test files for 15+ production modules is critically insufficient. The failing tests indicate the test suite provides false confidence. Score reflects near-complete absence of meaningful coverage on the most critical paths.

issues.summary: database.py is imported by 6 modules making it an uninsulated hub
evidence: database.py exports get_db_connection() and init_db(); both are imported directly by app.py, utils.py, files.py, scan.py, dump.py, and memprocfs.py
Each consumer opens/closes its own raw sqlite3 connection with no connection pool or context manager abstraction — any schema change to database.py forces review of all 6 callers
issues.suggestion: Introduce a repository or data-access layer (e.g. ScanRepository) in database.py that owns all queries; route handlers call repository methods rather than calling get_db_connection() directly. This shrinks the blast radius of schema changes to one file.

issues.summary: Two separate path-translation implementations for the same DooD concern
evidence: utils.py: resolve_host_path(container_path) reads HOST_PATH from env and translates using BASE_DIR; falls back through 'storage' substring heuristic
multi_volatility_base.py: resolve_path(path, host_path) takes host_path as argument and translates using BASE_DIR or CWD; different fallback logic
strings.py instantiates MultiVolatilityBase() at module scope solely to call _base.resolve_path(), bypassing utils.resolve_host_path()
issues.suggestion: Consolidate into one canonical resolve_host_path(path, host_path=None) function in a shared location (e.g. utils.py or a new paths.py). Remove the method from MultiVolatilityBase or have it delegate to the shared function. Update strings.py to call the shared function directly.

issues.summary: api_server/auth.py (before_request middleware) and routes/auth.py (login Blueprint) share the same conceptual name at adjacent package levels
evidence: api_server/auth.py exports check_authorization() — a before_request hook applied to all routes
routes/auth.py is a Blueprint exposing the /auth/login endpoint
A new engineer reading 'from multivol.api_server.auth import ...' and 'from multivol.api_server.routes.auth import ...' must read both files to understand the difference in role
issues.suggestion: Rename api_server/auth.py to api_server/auth_middleware.py (or auth_guard.py) to make the before_request role explicit and distinguish it from the login route blueprint.

issues.summary: run_analysis() is used as both a CLI entry point and an API callback, blurring the CLI/API boundary
evidence: multivol.py:main() calls run_analysis(args) directly for CLI use
app.py:run_api(runner_cb) receives run_analysis as a callback via init_runner(runner_cb)
run_analysis() creates os.getcwd()-relative directories (makedirs on volatility3_symbols etc) — CWD-sensitive logic that is inappropriate when called from an API server context
issues.suggestion: Separate a pure VolatilityRunner.run(config) method (no CWD assumptions, no console output) from the CLI wrapper in main(). API server passes VolatilityRunner.run; CLI wraps it with console output and CWD setup.

issues.summary: runner_func global in scan.py requires init_runner() to be called before any scan can execute — boot-order seam with no guard
evidence: scan.py line 66: runner_func: Optional[Callable[[argparse.Namespace], None]] = None
scan.py line 261-264: if runner_func: runner_func(args) else: logging.error('runner_func is None — scan cannot execute')
If init_runner() is not called (e.g. in a test or incomplete startup), the scan silently does nothing rather than raising an error
issues.suggestion: Replace the global with an app factory pattern: accept the runner as a constructor or factory argument rather than through a module-level setter. Alternatively, raise immediately if runner_func is None rather than silently skipping execution.

issues.summary: argparse.Namespace is constructed in route handlers and passed to the scan runner as the data carrier across the API/execution boundary
evidence: scan.py: args_dict is built from request.json, then converted to argparse.Namespace(**args_dict) and passed to runner_func
multivol.py:run_analysis() calls getattr(arguments, 'fetch_symbol', False) and getattr(arguments, 'debug', False) defensively because the namespace is sometimes missing fields
argparse.Namespace has no schema — any typo in the dict key silently creates a wrong attribute
issues.suggestion: Use Vol3RunConfig / Vol2RunConfig dataclasses (already defined in multi_volatility_base.py) as the API-to-runner carrier. Construct the dataclass directly in the route handler instead of going through argparse.Namespace.

issues.summary: list_evidences() is 130+ lines mixing two filesystem passes, two DB connections, and response assembly
evidence: Function opens/closes DB connection once to build case_map, then opens it again inside a loop for each _extracted directory to resolve source dump
Two separate loops over items (first pass: directories, second pass: files) with duplicated system-file filters
Nested function get_dir_size defined inside the route handler body
issues.suggestion: Extract get_dir_size to module level. Merge the two DB queries into one at the top of the function. Extract the _extracted-dir handling into a helper build_extracted_group(item, case_map). This reduces the function to a coordinator of 3-4 well-named helpers.

issues.summary: background_dump_task() is 110+ lines mixing Docker orchestration, path setup, file management, and DB updates
evidence: Function constructs vol command, creates directories, starts Docker container, moves output files, and updates dump_tasks and DB — five distinct concerns in one function body

try/except/finally with variables (created_files, case_extract_dir) set in the try block and referenced in the finally block — if an exception is raised before case_extract_dir is set, the finally block would raise NameError
issues.suggestion: Extract build_vol_command(scan, virt_addr, file_path), run_vol_container(cmd, volumes, image_tag), and move_output_files(src_dir, dst_dir) as standalone helpers. The main function becomes a coordinator calling these helpers inside the try block.

issues.summary: clean_and_parse_json annotated as Union[list, dict] but tests assert it returns None for a missing file
evidence: utils.py: def clean_and_parse_json(filepath: str) -> Union[list, dict]
When the file does not exist the function returns {"error": "File not found", "raw_output": ""} — a dict, not None
test_utils.py: assert result is None — this assertion will fail at runtime because the actual return is a dict
issues.suggestion: Fix the test to assert result == {"error": "File not found", "raw_output": ""}. The annotation Union[list, dict] is accurate; the test was written against a different contract.

issues.summary: resolve_host_path annotated as -> str but test asserts it returns None for an unknown path
evidence: utils.py: def resolve_host_path(container_path: str) -> str — always returns a string (falls back to container_path)
test_utils.py: test_resolve_host_path_returns_none_for_missing asserts result is None
Runtime returns '/nonexistent_scan_id_xyz' (the original input) when HOST_PATH is not set
issues.suggestion: Fix the test: remove the None assertion and instead check that the fallback returns the input unchanged (assert result == '/nonexistent_scan_id_xyz'). The str return type annotation is correct.

issues.summary: Most Flask route handlers and the run_analysis function lack type annotations
evidence: multivol.py: def run_analysis(arguments): — no annotation on arguments; the function accepts argparse.Namespace but callers can pass anything
scan.py: def background_scan(s_id, args): — no annotations
vol3_wrapper(packed_args) and vol2_wrapper(packed_args) have no annotations despite being called via multiprocessing.Pool
issues.suggestion: Annotate run_analysis(arguments: argparse.Namespace) -> None. Annotate background_scan(s_id: str, args: argparse.Namespace) -> None. Consider replacing argparse.Namespace with the Vol3RunConfig/Vol2RunConfig dataclasses for stronger guarantees.

issues.summary: utils.py uses two different error strategies for I/O failures: get_file_hash raises OSError while clean_and_parse_json returns an error dict
evidence: get_file_hash(): docstring says 'Raises OSError if the file cannot be read'; calculate_sha256() propagates FileNotFoundError from open()
clean_and_parse_json(): returns {"error": "File not found", "raw_output": ""} when file is missing instead of raising
Callers of both functions must use completely different error-handling patterns even though both are reading files
issues.suggestion: Decide on one strategy for the module. Since callers are Flask routes, returning error dicts is more idiomatic for utils used at the HTTP layer. Either (a) have get_file_hash return None on failure and document it, or (b) have clean_and_parse_json raise FileNotFoundError on missing file. Apply the chosen strategy consistently.

issues.summary: memprocfs_server.py swallows all exceptions in VFS listing and wait_for_forensic with bare except + pass
evidence: list_files_recursive: except Exception: return — silently returns no results if VFS listing fails
wait_for_forensic: except Exception: pass — loop continues silently if vmm.vfs.list throws; no logging of what failed
These silent failures mean a broken VMM state produces empty results with no diagnosable error
issues.suggestion: Add logging.warning() or print() inside the exception handlers with the exception message. In list_files_recursive, propagate the exception up or at minimum log it before returning early. In wait_for_forensic, log the exception and increment a failure counter.

issues.summary: /health endpoint is not exempted from auth in check_authorization, but test asserts it returns 200 without a token
evidence: auth.py check_authorization(): exempts request.path == '/auth/login' only; /health is not exempted
Confirmed at runtime: GET /health without token returns HTTP 401
test_auth.py: test_health_check_bypasses_auth asserts resp.status_code == 200 — this test fails
issues.suggestion: Add 'or request.path == "/health"' to the exempt paths in check_authorization(). The health endpoint is a standard liveness probe that monitoring systems call without credentials.

issues.summary: check_authorization() has three distinct return shapes that callers cannot distinguish by type
evidence: Returns None when auth passes (Flask before_request: no response)
Returns ('', 200) tuple for OPTIONS preflight
Returns (jsonify({"error": "Unauthorized"}), 401) tuple for failed auth
Flask before_request treats any non-None return as the response, so all three are valid — but the signature conveys nothing about this
issues.suggestion: Add a return type annotation: -> Optional[tuple]. Add a docstring explaining the three outcomes. This is not a bug but the lack of annotation makes the contract invisible.

issues.summary: Scan routes mix /status/<scan_id> and /scans/<uuid> URL patterns for the same resource
evidence: /scan (POST) to create — no ID in path
/status/<scan_id> (GET) to read status — uses 'scan_id' parameter name
/scans/<uuid> (GET, PUT, DELETE) — uses 'uuid' parameter name, different prefix
/scans/<uuid>/modules, /scans/<uuid>/download, /scans/<uuid>/execute — all use 'uuid' naming
issues.suggestion: Normalize to /scans (POST), /scans/<scan_id> (GET, PUT, DELETE) throughout. Rename the /status/<scan_id> route to /scans/<scan_id>/status or merge its response into /scans/<scan_id>.

issues.summary: run_analysis() mixes Docker image check, CLI argument resolution, multiprocessing setup, progress display, and result reporting in one 200+ line function
evidence: Lines 32-37: makedirs for 4 directories with os.getcwd()-based paths
Lines 105-129: Docker image existence check and pull with rich Progress spinner
Lines 138-277: multiprocessing.Pool setup, task construction, result collection with console output
Lines 182-207: priority execution of info_module before the pool — hidden sequencing logic buried mid-function
issues.suggestion: Extract check_and_pull_image(client, image_name, console), resolve_commands(arguments, runner_instance), and run_pool_vol3(tasks, max_processes, console) as standalone functions. run_analysis() becomes a coordinator that calls these in sequence.

issues.summary: execute_plugin route in scan.py has acknowledged incomplete design with inline ingestion logic that should be in utils
evidence: Comment in execute_plugin at background_single_run: '# Need a quick local ingest instead of the huge helper due to scope changes. # Actually I'd rather move ingestion to utils entirely but I'll write logic to load JSON and write DB block.'
ingest_results_to_db() in the same file (line ~80) already handles this; execute_plugin duplicates a subset of it inline
issues.suggestion: Replace the inline ingestion in background_single_run with a call to ingest_results_to_db(scan_id, output_dir). The current code is a known shortcut that creates two diverging ingestion paths.

issues.summary: check_authorization() is named like a predicate/validator but returns None or a Flask response tuple — callers cannot treat it as a boolean
evidence: Function registered as app.before_request(check_authorization) — Flask convention for before_request hooks returning None (proceed) or response (abort)
Name 'check_authorization' implies it only checks — it also generates and returns HTTP responses
Named after the verb 'check' which conventionally means 'return bool' in Python
issues.suggestion: Rename to authorize_request() to make clear it is an active guard that either passes through (returns None) or responds (returns tuple). This matches Flask before_request hook semantics.

issues.summary: Some route handlers use request.json (attribute) while others use request.get_json() (method) — inconsistent across sibling routes
evidence: scan.py: data = request.json
dump.py: data = request.json
routes/auth.py: data = request.get_json()
The two forms behave differently: request.get_json() returns None on parse failure and supports silent=True; request.json raises 400 if Content-Type is not JSON in newer Flask versions
issues.suggestion: Standardize on request.get_json() with a None guard throughout all route handlers for defensive behavior. Replace all request.json direct attribute accesses.

issues.summary: strings.py creates a MultiVolatilityBase() instance at module scope solely for two utility methods
evidence: strings.py line 6: _base = MultiVolatilityBase()
The instance is used only to call _base.resolve_path() and _base.safe_print()
MultiVolatilityBase has no state — __init__ is empty (pass) — so the class adds no value over module-level functions
issues.suggestion: Extract resolve_path() and safe_print() as module-level functions in a shared utils module (or multi_volatility_base.py at module level). strings.py then imports and calls them directly without instantiating a class.

issues.summary: config.py captures API_TOKEN, APP_PASSWORD, and STORAGE_DIR at import time; tests that set env vars after import get stale values
evidence: config.py: API_TOKEN = os.getenv('API_TOKEN', 'multivol_default_secret_token') — evaluated once at module import
config.py: APP_PASSWORD = os.getenv('APP_PASSWORD', 'multivol_password')
conftest.py sets os.environ before importing the app, so this works only by accident of import order; any test that imports config.py before setting env vars will use the default weak token
issues.suggestion: Wrap env var reads in functions: def get_api_token() -> str: return os.getenv('API_TOKEN', 'multivol_default_secret_token'). In auth.py, call get_api_token() per-request instead of importing the module-level constant.

issues.summary: MCP module creates an httpx.AsyncClient with API_TOKEN at module import time; token changes after import are not reflected
evidence: MCP/multivol_mcp.py: API_TOKEN = os.getenv('API_TOKEN') — captured once
client = httpx.AsyncClient(headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {API_TOKEN}'}, ...) — created at module scope with the captured value
If the container is restarted with a different API_TOKEN env var, the process must be restarted; more critically, if API_TOKEN is unset, the client sends 'Bearer None' silently
issues.suggestion: Create the httpx client lazily (on first request) or pass the token per-request: use default_headers only after the env var has been validated non-None. Add a startup check that raises if API_TOKEN is not set.

issues.summary: requirements.txt declares only pyyaml and requests; at least 7 additional runtime dependencies are imported but undeclared
evidence: requirements.txt: pyyaml, requests — 2 packages only
app.py imports: flask, flask_cors, waitress
multi_volatility2.py and multi_volatility3.py import: docker
multivol.py imports: rich
MCP/multivol_mcp.py imports: httpx, fastmcp
setup.py install_requires reads from requirements.txt — so pip install multivol installs an unusable package
issues.suggestion: Add all runtime dependencies to requirements.txt: flask, flask-cors, docker, waitress, rich, httpx, fastmcp. Pin minimum versions where known. Consider splitting into requirements.txt (core CLI), requirements-api.txt (API server), and requirements-mcp.txt (MCP server).

issues.summary: execute_plugin in scan.py contains a self-narrating development comment that documents intent rather than decision
evidence: Comment in background_single_run: '# Need a quick local ingest instead of the huge helper due to scope changes. # Actually I\'d rather move ingestion to utils entirely but I\'ll write logic to load JSON and write DB block.'
This comment describes the developer\'s thought process, not the code\'s behavior or constraints; it acknowledges a shortcut without documenting why the shortcut is acceptable long-term
issues.suggestion: Replace with a TODO: '# TODO: replace with ingest_results_to_db() call once execute_plugin output_dir semantics are aligned' and add a ticket reference. Or better, implement the TODO immediately — the fix is straightforward (see design_coherence finding).

issues.summary: CLI/build/lib/multivol/api.py is an 1838-line monolithic API file from a prior architecture that still exists in the repository
evidence: CLI/build/lib/multivol/api.py: 1838 lines; imports Flask, creates a single app with all routes inline
CLI/build/lib/multivol/api_strings_extension.py: 87 lines; separate strings endpoint module from old architecture
Current architecture uses CLI/multivol/api_server/ with separate blueprints — a clean replacement
build/lib/ contains an older version of all modules including multi_volatility2.py, multi_volatility3.py
issues.suggestion: Delete the CLI/build/ directory entirely. It should not be in version control; add CLI/build/ to .gitignore. The old api.py provides no reference value that the current blueprint structure does not already express.

issues.summary: Three tests in test_utils.py assert return values that match an older API contract, not the current implementation
evidence: test_clean_and_parse_json_missing_file: assert result is None — actual return is {"error": "File not found", "raw_output": ""}
test_resolve_host_path_returns_none_for_missing: assert result is None — actual return is the original path string
These tests were written for a version of the API where these functions returned None on failure; the functions were later changed to return error dicts / fall back to the input
issues.suggestion: Fix the three tests to match current behavior. test_clean_and_parse_json_missing_file: assert 'error' in result. test_resolve_host_path_returns_none_for_missing: assert result == '/nonexistent_scan_id_xyz'. Run pytest to confirm all tests pass after the fix.

issues.summary: commands variable in run_analysis() is unbound if arguments.mode is neither 'vol2' nor 'vol3'
evidence: lines 50-83: commands is assigned inside 'if arguments.mode == "vol2":' and 'elif arguments.mode == "vol3":' blocks only
line 95: max_processes = min(max_procs, len(commands)) — NameError: name 'commands' is not defined if mode is unexpected
Confirmed by test: python3 -c 'mode="unknown"; ... len(commands)' raises NameError
From the API, mode is validated to 'vol2'/'vol3' before calling run_analysis — but if called from other contexts, this is a latent bug
issues.suggestion: Add a guard: 'else: raise ValueError(f"Unsupported mode: {arguments.mode}")' after the elif block, or initialize commands = [] before the if block and raise after if commands is still empty.

issues.summary: /health endpoint requires authorization — monitoring systems and load balancers cannot probe it without a token
evidence: auth.py check_authorization() only exempts request.path == '/auth/login'
Runtime verification: GET /health without Authorization header returns HTTP 401
test_auth.py test_health_check_bypasses_auth expects status 200 — test fails
Standard practice: health/liveness endpoints are unauthenticated
issues.suggestion: Add '/health' to the exempt paths in check_authorization(): if request.path in ('/auth/login', '/health'): return None. Add a corresponding test to confirm.

issues.summary: memprocfs_server.py exposes /init, /list, /read, /shutdown with no authentication
evidence: memprocfs_server.py has no before_request auth hook and no token checks on any endpoint
/read endpoint serves raw file bytes from the memory dump VFS without any access control
/shutdown endpoint can terminate the sidecar process without authentication
The sidecar is expected to run on a private Docker network — but any container on that network can access arbitrary files from the memory dump
issues.suggestion: Add a shared secret token (passed as Docker environment variable) to the sidecar. Add a simple before_request check comparing the Authorization header to the token. Alternatively, bind the sidecar to localhost only and route all requests through the main API.

issues.summary: Default API_TOKEN and APP_PASSWORD fall back to hard-coded weak values that users may never change
evidence: config.py: API_TOKEN = os.getenv('API_TOKEN', 'multivol_default_secret_token')
config.py: APP_PASSWORD = os.getenv('APP_PASSWORD', 'multivol_password')
If users run the server without setting env vars, these well-known defaults are the active credentials
issues.suggestion: Remove the fallback defaults. Instead, raise a startup error if API_TOKEN or APP_PASSWORD are not set: 'if not API_TOKEN: raise RuntimeError("API_TOKEN environment variable must be set")'.

issues.summary: Core execution logic — Volatility runners, Docker management, scan orchestration — has zero test coverage
evidence: Only two test files exist: tests/test_auth.py (4 tests) and tests/test_utils.py (6 tests)
execute_command_volatility3() and execute_command_volatility2() — the core scan execution functions — have no tests
background_dump_task(), background_scan() — async task functions with complex error handling — have no tests
The 6 route blueprints (docker, files, scan, dump, memprocfs, auth) have no route-level tests
MCP tools in multivol_mcp.py have no tests
issues.suggestion: Add integration tests using the Flask test_client (fixtures already in conftest.py) for: (1) POST /scan validates missing fields, (2) GET /status/<id> returns correct state, (3) GET /evidences returns correct structure. For runners, add unit tests that mock docker.from_env() to test command construction without Docker. Target 80% coverage of route handlers as a first milestone.

issues.summary: Three tests in test_utils.py assert incorrect return values and will fail on any test run
evidence: test_clean_and_parse_json_missing_file: assert result is None — actual: {'error': 'File not found', 'raw_output': ''}
test_resolve_host_path_returns_none_for_missing: assert result is None — actual: '/nonexistent_scan_id_xyz'
test_health_check_bypasses_auth (in test_auth.py): assert resp.status_code == 200 — actual: 401
These failures were confirmed by running the test setup manually
issues.suggestion: Fix test_clean_and_parse_json_missing_file to assert 'error' in result. Fix test_resolve_host_path_returns_none_for_missing to assert result == '/nonexistent_scan_id_xyz'. Fix test_health_check_bypasses_auth by first fixing the /health auth exemption (see authorization_consistency finding).

issues.summary: CLI/build/lib/multivol/ contains old production code that should not be in version control
evidence: CLI/build/ is a setuptools build artifact directory containing duplicates of the source tree
build/lib/multivol/api.py is 1838 lines — old monolith; current src has api_server/ with blueprints
Any developer navigating CLI/multivol/ will also find CLI/build/lib/multivol/ via search tools — creates confusion about which is canonical
issues.suggestion: Add CLI/build/ to .gitignore. Delete the directory from version control. Run git rm -r CLI/build/ --cached to remove it from tracking without deleting the local files (which are build artifacts).
